cataloging_subject.label: St. Brandan: A Medieval Legend of the Sea, in English Verse and Prose
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the U-M/General Corpus edition-level selection encoded from Thomas Wright's 1844 Percy Society publication: its title-leaf content, the 732-line Metrical Life of St. Brandan on printed pp. 1-34, and the Prose Life of St. Brandan on pp. 35-56. The two lives are distinct textual versions with distinct immediate sources. The selection excludes Wright's half-title, Percy Society council list, preface on pp. v-viii, and notes on pp. 57-63; it is neither one medieval work nor one manuscript witness.

catalog_summary.title: St. Brandan: A Medieval Legend of the Sea, in English Verse and Prose
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous English authors or adapters of two distinct textual versions
catalog_summary.date: Metrical Life composed 1250-1290; selected prose source printed 27 August 1527; edited selection published 1844
catalog_summary.region: England; no single more precise place of composition is established for both texts

titles.value: St. Brandan: A Medieval Legend of the Sea, in English Verse and Prose

assertion.notes: The title is the edition-level wrapper for the two selected lives, not a shared medieval title.

Here begynneth the lyfe of saynt Brandon

notes: A cataloging collective for the unnamed persons responsible for the metrical and prose versions; it does not assert common authorship or a direct relationship between the two English texts.

notes: 1810-1877; editor and compiler of the 1844 Percy Society selection.

The metrical text printed from British Library Harley MS 2277
Selected readings from Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.25

assertion.notes: Wright describes an earlier collation and prints only two or three selected readings; R.3.25 is not modeled as a coequal base text.
A specialist composition range for the poem, kept distinct from the circa-1300 date and South-West English production of Harley MS 2277.

date_statements.display: Attested by 27 August 1527 in the printed source used by Wright; earlier states are not traced in this record

scope.description: A terminus supplied by the selected printed source, not a composition date or a claim that 1527 was the prose version's first printing

places.label: England
notes: Used only for a broad cultural-origin statement; witness production, dialect, and publication places are not transferred to both works.

assertion.notes: The two texts belong to English-language traditions, but the evidence does not establish one precise place of composition for the selection.
The top-level XML LANG='enm' is too broad for this prose part; its 1527 printed language is modeled separately.

scope.description: Edition title, headings, and retained editorial presentation

form_statements.description: The edition-level selection juxtaposes one verse life and one prose life.
A 732-line poem in septenary rhymed couplets.
A prose saint's life extracted from the 1527 Golden Legend.

subject_statements.label: Terrestrial Paradise and the Land of Promise

summaries.value: Two English versions recount Brendan's seven-year voyage with a company of monks through a sacred and marvelous ocean landscape. They encounter providential islands and communities, the whale Jasconius, birds identified with fallen angels, demons and Judas, hermits, and finally the Land of Promise before Brendan returns to Ireland and dies.
The XML contains a title-page division followed by two complete content divisions: the 732-line Metrical Life on printed pp. 1-34 and the Prose Life in fifteen encoded paragraphs on pp. 35-56.

The record describes the encoded edition-level selection. Wright printed the verse from Harley MS 2277 and cited a limited earlier collation against Trinity R.3.25; he took the prose text from Wynkyn de Worde's 1527 Golden Legend. Preface and notes are evidence for the record but are outside the encoded selection.

Textual, physical, editorial, and digital layers are kept separate. Harley 2277 is the verse base witness; R.3.25 is a selectively collated witness; the Golden Legend is the prose source edition; Wright's publication is the immediate edition; U-M is a digital encoding; and the checked-in XML is the repository artifact. The South-Western dialect and circa-1300 date of Harley 2277 are not assigned to the entire edited selection.

extent.description: The XML has three DIV1 elements: one title-page wrapper and two content works. The metrical division has one LG and 732 L elements; the prose division has 15 P elements. Across the file there are 57 PB elements and two HEAD elements. These are encoding and edition metrics, not evidence that the two lives share authorship or an immediate source.

content_parts.label: The Metrical Life of St. Brandan
content_parts.summary: A Middle English verse life in which Brendan and his monks voyage through marvelous islands and infernal and paradisal spaces before returning to Ireland.
notes: Trinity R.3.25 ff. 215r-222v witnesses the poem but is omitted from the locators because Wright used it only for a few selected collations; the lineage binding records that qualified role.

content_parts.label: Prose Life of St. Brandan
content_parts.summary: An Early Modern English prose life of Brendan and his companions' seven-year voyage, extracted by Wright from the 1527 Golden Legend.

related_works.title: South English Legendary
notes: The metrical life circulates as a component of the South English Legendary; this does not make Wright's prose selection part of that compilation.

related_works.title: The Golden Legend (Wynkyn de Worde, 1527)
notes: The prose life is a component excerpted from this printed collection.

evidence.citation: Thomas Wright, ed., St. Brandan: A Medieval Legend of the Sea, in English Verse and Prose (London: printed for the Percy Society by T. Richards, 1844), complete standalone and bound-volume scans.
evidence.locator: standalone scan leaves 6-76; bound-volume scan leaves 111-181; selected text at printed pp. 1-56
evidence.summary: Immediate-edition evidence for title, publication, Wright's responsibility, the full verse and prose texts, page boundaries, and excluded apparatus.
notes: The SHA-256 is for the cached standalone PDF; the lineage manifest separately records the complete bound-volume PDF.

evidence.citation: Thomas Wright, 'Preface' and 'Notes to the Metrical Life,' in St. Brandan (1844), pp. v-viii and 57-63.
evidence.summary: Wright's explicit statement that the verse was printed from Harley MS 2277, that a few readings came from an earlier collation of Trinity R.3.25, and that the prose was taken from the 1527 Golden Legend.

evidence.citation: Parsed Corpus of Middle English Poetry, Saint Brendan text details.
evidence.summary: Specialist evidence for poem identity, 1250-1290 composition range with circa 1260 suggestion, 732-line septenary couplet form, DIMEV/IMEV identifiers, and the Harley 2277 locus ff. 41v-51r.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 2277, record 040-002048108.
evidence.summary: Official evidence for the verse base witness's shelfmark, circa-1300 date, South-West England production, physical description, and South English Legendary context; the catalog currently marks images unavailable.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, James Catalogue of Western Manuscripts, R.3.25 (James 605), South English Legendary.
evidence.summary: Official evidence for the correctly resolved collated shelfmark R.3.25, fifteenth-century parchment codex, physical extent, and Saint Brandan beginning at f. 215r.
notes: Direct inspection of Wright's preface confirms R.3.25; OCR variants R.3.26 and R.8.25 are erroneous.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, R.3.25 IIIF Presentation manifest and complete 586-canvas facsimile.
evidence.locator: Saint Brandan at ff. 215r-222v, canvases 461-476; Saint Patrick begins f. 223r
evidence.summary: Direct-image evidence for the collated text boundary and complete publicly available manuscript surrogate under CC BY-NC 4.0.

evidence.citation: The legende, named in Latyn Legenda aurea that is to say in Englysshe The golden legende (London: Wynkyn de Worde, 27 August 1527), Boston Public Library G.527.J15L, Internet Archive item legendenamedinla00jaco_0.
evidence.locator: Saint Brandon at printed ff. CCCXLIIIr-CCCXLVIIr, scan leaves 807-815
evidence.summary: Complete-source evidence for the prose text extracted by Wright, its original printed heading, 1527 publication statement, and boundaries within the larger Golden Legend.

evidence.citation: Structural inspection of CME00019.xml with XPath element counts and repository file hashing.
evidence.locator: count(//DIV1), count(//HEAD), count(//LG), count(//L), count(//P), count(//PB), TEXT/@LANG, and SHA-256
evidence.summary: Reproducibly finds 3 DIV1, 2 HEAD, 1 LG, 732 L, 15 P, and 57 PB elements, with top-level TEXT/@LANG='enm'; that language attribute is not treated as sufficient classification for the 1527 prose part.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for CME00019.
evidence.summary: Linked evidence for the separation and qualified relationships among repository XML, U-M encoding, Wright edition, two manuscript witnesses, 1527 prose source edition, complete facsimiles, access routes, and layer-specific rights statements.

open_questions.question: What is the exact textual relationship and direction of dependence between the metrical and prose English versions, and what are their respective relationships to the wider Brendan tradition?
next_steps: Consult current specialist scholarship on the South English Legendary Brendan and the English Golden Legend tradition.
Collate representative narrative units without assuming that broad plot agreement proves direct dependence.
Keep the two parts modeled as distinct works until a specific source route is evidenced.
notes: Wright explicitly declined to determine which English version represented the original more faithfully.

open_questions.question: When and by whom was the particular English prose Brendan version printed in the 1527 Golden Legend first composed or translated?
next_steps: Trace the prose text through earlier Caxton and de Worde Golden Legend editions.
Consult bibliographical and textual scholarship on the English Golden Legend translation and revision history.
Keep 1527 scoped to Wright's printed source rather than treating it as a composition date.

open_questions.question: Why does the XML title leaf end 'AUGUST, 1844. No. XLVIII.' while both inspected scans of the title page end 'M.DCCC.XLIV.'?
next_steps: Ask U-M whether the encoding followed a wrapper, alternate issue, proof, or secondary source not represented by the inspected copies.
Inspect additional physical or digitized copies for issue-specific title leaves.
Retain the XML wording as encoded while documenting the discrepancy.

open_questions.question: Can a complete current color facsimile of British Library Harley MS 2277 be obtained to verify the base-text boundary and Wright's readings directly?
next_steps: Recheck the British Library catalog and digitized-manuscript recovery status.
Request a complete physical-object reproduction through British Library Digitisation Services, including covers, flyleaves, blanks, all folios, and adjacent works.
Map the Saint Brendan portion at ff. 41v-51r separately after receipt and record supplied-file rights before caching.
notes: The former British Library digitized-manuscript ARK is recorded in the lineage manifest, but neither that host nor the legacy IIIF route yielded a current complete public copy.
The preferred title describes Wright's 1844 edited selection, not a single medieval work title.
The metrical and prose lives are cataloged as distinct content parts with independent language, form, date, and source statements.

Harley MS 2277 is the metrical base witness; Trinity R.3.25 is only a selectively collated witness. OCR readings R.3.26 and R.8.25 are rejected after direct inspection of Wright's preface and the Trinity catalogs.
The 1527 Golden Legend is an immediate printed source for Wright's prose selection, not a manuscript witness and not proof that the underlying prose version originated in 1527.
The record remains partial because the inter-version source relationship, prose version history, title-leaf discrepancy, and direct base-witness verification remain unresolved.
